1: “The word of YHWH was rare in those days; there was no frequent vision (ḥāzôn nip̄rāṣ).” The silence of YHWH’s word — the prophetic famine — frames the pre-Samuel era and creates the context for his call. John 1:14 (“And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us”) is the ultimate end of every prophetic silence: the logos that was “rare” and partial throughout the OT period became fully and permanently present in the incarnation. Hebrews 1:1-2 identifies this directly: “Long ago, at many times and in many ways, God spoke to our fathers by the prophets, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son.” Samuel ends the current prophetic silence; Christ ends all prophetic silence permanently.
2: Eli’s dim and failing eyes — he could no longer see (kēhôṯ ʿênāyw lōʾ yûḵal lirʾôṯ) — are the physical embodiment of the spiritual condition the narrator has been describing: the official religious leadership that cannot see what YHWH is doing. The prophetic blindness of the establishment that cannot perceive the new thing YHWH is creating is the pattern Jesus identifies in the Pharisees: “you are blind guides” (Matthew 23:16, 24); “having eyes they do not see” (Mark 8:18). The failing vision of the old priestly order is the backdrop against which the new prophetic clarity that Samuel will embody is introduced.
3: “The lamp of God had not yet gone out, and Samuel was lying down in the temple of the LORD, near the ark of God.” The lamp burning all night (Exodus 27:20-21) near the ark — YHWH’s presence — is the last light of the old order, still burning but nearly exhausted. Samuel lies at the threshold of this dying light and is about to receive the new prophetic word. John 1:5 uses the same imagery of light in darkness: “The light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it.” Christ is the light that the darkness — the prophetic lamp that never goes out — because he is not merely an intermediary of the divine word but the word himself.
4: “Then YHWH called, ‘Samuel!’ and he said, ‘Here I am’ (hinnēnî).” The hinnēnî response — “here I am”, the formula of complete covenant availability — is used by Abraham (Genesis 22:1), Moses (Exodus 3:4), and Isaiah (Isaiah 6:8). Samuel’s hinnēnî in response to what he thinks is Eli’s voice is still the right response: total availability, no precondition. Christ’s “not my will but yours be done” (Luke 22:42) is the ultimate hinnēnî — the complete covenant availability of the Son to the Father’s purpose, even when that purpose includes the cross.
5: Samuel runs to Eli saying “Here I am, for you called me” — but Eli says he did not call. The threefold misidentification (vv5-8) before correct identification is the OT pattern of prophetic formation: the call must be recognized through a process of discernment. The disciples’ misidentification of Jesus throughout the Gospel narratives — mistaking him for a ghost on the water (Matthew 14:26), not understanding his predictions of death and resurrection — is the same formation pattern. Understanding who calls and what the call means is the work of a lifetime of discipleship, not immediate comprehension.
6: YHWH calls Samuel again; Samuel runs to Eli again. The second misidentification deepens the pattern without altering Samuel’s posture: his hinnēnî (here I am) and his running toward what he thinks is the source of the call are correct even when the identification is wrong. The persistence of the call and the persistence of the response despite misunderstanding is the structure of covenant pursuit: YHWH calls, the called one responds, the relationship deepens through the very process of discernment. Christ’s disciples repeatedly misunderstood his teaching (Mark 9:32: “they did not understand the saying, and were afraid to ask him”) while still following — the running without full understanding that characterizes early discipleship.
7: “Now Samuel did not yet know YHWH, and the word of YHWH had not yet been revealed to him.” The not-yet-knowing is circumstantial — it will be remedied in this very night — in contrast to the sons of Eli whose not-knowing (1 Samuel 2:12) was permanent and culpable. The theology of progressive revelation: there is a “not yet” in the history of YHWH’s self-disclosure that is not failure but preparation. The disciples “did not understand any of these things” about Christ’s death and resurrection “until these things happened” (Luke 18:34; 1 Samuel 24:8) — the not-yet of genuine progressive understanding being overcome by the Spirit (John 16:13: “he will guide you into all truth”).
8: YHWH calls Samuel a third time; Eli perceives that YHWH is calling the boy. The pattern of three calls before recognition echoes throughout sacred narrative: Peter’s threefold denial is reversed in Christ’s threefold “do you love me?” (John 21:15-17), restoring Peter to exactly the three-count denial. The number three in OT call narratives (three calls before identification) corresponds to the three-day pattern of death and resurrection — the process of dying to old misidentification and rising to true recognition. Eli’s eventual discernment, however dim his eyes, points to the moment when full recognition arrives.
9: Eli instructs Samuel: “Go, lie down, and if he calls you, you shall say, ‘Speak, YHWH, for your servant hears.’” The response-formula Eli prescribes — “speak, YHWH, for your servant hears” — is the model of receptive obedience to divine speech. Isaiah’s response (Isaiah 6:8: “Here I am; send me”) and Mary’s (Luke 1:38: “Let it be to me according to your word”) are the supreme NT expressions of this posture. Christ’s entire earthly life is the living expression of “speak, Father, for your servant hears”: “I have come down from heaven not to do my own will but the will of him who sent me” (John 6:38).
10: YHWH comes and stands, calling as before: “Samuel! Samuel!” — the doubled name of urgent divine address. Samuel responds: “Speak, for your servant hears” — the learned formula, now properly directed to YHWH. The theophanic appearance (YHWH comes and stands, wayyāḇōʾ wayyiṯyaṣṣēḇ) is the personal divine presence that initiates the prophetic relationship. Saul’s Damascus road encounter with the risen Christ (Acts 9:4: “Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me?”) uses the same doubled-name formula. The risen Christ who stops Saul in his tracks and initiates his prophetic call is the YHWH who stood and called Samuel — the same divine person in the fullness of revelation.
11: “Behold, I am about to do something in Israel at which the two ears of everyone who hears it will tingle.” The ear-tingling oracle announces a divine act of such severity that it will be received as a physical shock by every hearer. Christ’s announcement of Jerusalem’s destruction (Matthew 24:2: “not one stone will be left on another”) produces the same stunned response from his disciples (v3: “when will these things be?”). The prophetic pattern: YHWH announces the historically unthinkable before it occurs, so that when it happens, the divine word is validated and YHWH’s sovereign foreknowledge is demonstrated.
12: “On that day I will carry out against Eli everything I have spoken concerning his house, from beginning to end.” The total fulfillment of the prophetic word — nothing held back, nothing softened — is YHWH’s covenant faithfulness in judgment as well as blessing. The consistency of divine word and historical event is the basis for prophetic authority. Peter applies this principle to Christ’s death: “this Jesus, delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God, you crucified and killed by the hands of lawless men” (Acts 2:23) — what YHWH spoke through the prophets concerning the suffering of the Messiah was carried out from beginning to end.
13: “I have told him that I am about to judge his house forever for the iniquity that he knew, because his sons were blaspheming God, and he did not restrain them.” The charge is not that Eli sinned as his sons did but that he knew and did not act. The passive complicity of the one who knows and fails to restrain is itself covenant unfaithfulness. James 4:17: “whoever knows the right thing to do and fails to do it, for him it is sin.” Christ’s faithfulness in contrast: he not only knew what was happening in the temple and spoke against it, he also acted — the cleansing is knowledge acted upon.
14: “The iniquity of Eli’s house shall never be atoned for by sacrifice or offering forever.” The impossibility of atoning for this particular sin by the very system his sons corrupted points to the limits of the Levitical sacrificial system — limits that the book of Hebrews develops systematically. Hebrews 10:4: “it is impossible for the blood of bulls and goats to take away sins.” The sin that the Levitical system cannot atone requires the better sacrifice — Christ’s once-for-all offering (Hebrews 10:10-14) — that accomplishes what the sacrificial system could only gesture toward.
15: Samuel lay until morning and opened the sanctuary doors — his regular duties performed without alteration despite the overwhelming oracle received. He is afraid to tell Eli the vision. The faithful prophet who carries a difficult word and feels its weight — afraid to speak it to the one it concerns — is the prototype of every NT messenger who bears the gospel’s both-difficult-and-glorious content. Paul: “I am not ashamed of the gospel” (Romans 1:16) — the explicit overcoming of the fear Samuel feels, enabled by the resurrection’s final reversal of the devastating pattern that Eli represents.
16: Eli calls “Samuel, my son!” — the paternal address that invokes the relationship built over years of sanctuary service. Samuel responds hinnēnî — the same availability formula he used with YHWH. His responsiveness to both divine and human authority, using the same formula, shows the integration of vertical and horizontal covenant loyalty. Christ’s honoring of both his Father’s authority and legitimate human authority (Luke 2:51: “he went down with them and was submissive to them”) — while maintaining the priority of the Father’s business (1 Samuel 2:49) — models the same integrated covenant posture.
17: Eli’s covenant oath — “God do so to you and more also if you hide anything from me” — legally binds Samuel to full disclosure. The self-imprecation oath that compels complete truth-telling is the instrument that ensures YHWH’s word is transmitted without softening. Christ’s “But I say to you, do not swear at all... let what you say be simply ‘yes’ or ‘no’” (Matthew 5:34,37) addresses a different issue — casual oath-swearing — but the underlying principle is the same: total truthfulness in speech that needs no oath to enforce it. Christ himself speaks with the authority that needs no oath (“truly I say to you”) because his word is self-authenticating.
18: Samuel tells Eli everything and hides nothing. Eli’s response: “He is YHWH. Let him do what seems good to him.” The theological submission — accepting YHWH’s judgment as “good” even when devastating — is Eli’s finest moment and his last significant speech. Gethsemane: “not my will but yours be done” (Luke 22:42) is the perfected form of Eli’s submission. The difference: Christ’s submission is not passive acceptance of deserved judgment but active embrace of undeserved suffering for the salvation of others — the same verbal formula expressing infinitely different content.
19: “Samuel grew, and YHWH was with him and let none of his words fall to the ground (nāpal ʾārṣāh).” The prophetic reliability — every word fulfilled, none falling without accomplishing its purpose — is the prophetic standard (Deuteronomy 18:22) that Samuel meets from his first oracle. Isaiah 55:11 applies this principle to YHWH’s word itself: “it shall not return to me empty, but it shall accomplish that which I purpose.” Christ is himself the word of YHWH that accomplishes its complete purpose — not one jot or tittle will pass from the law until all is accomplished (Matthew 5:18); his words will not pass away (Matthew 24:35).
20: “And all Israel from Dan to Beersheba knew that Samuel had been established as a prophet (nāḇîʾ) of YHWH.” The national recognition of Samuel’s prophetic authority follows directly from YHWH’s establishment of his words. The nation’s acknowledgment is secondary; YHWH’s establishment is primary. Christ’s authority is similarly established by the Father before and independent of human recognition: “You are my beloved Son” (Mark 1:11; Matthew 17:5) is the divine establishment that precedes and grounds any human acknowledgment. The crowd’s recognition of Jesus’s authority (Matthew 7:28-29: “he taught them as one who had authority”) follows from YHWH’s prior establishment.
21: “And YHWH continued to appear at Shiloh, for YHWH revealed himself to Samuel at Shiloh through the word of YHWH.” The sustained prophetic revelation at Shiloh through Samuel marks the end of the prophetic silence and the beginning of the age of classical prophecy. The triple revelation vocabulary — appeared, revealed, word — describes the comprehensive self-disclosure. John 1:14-18 is the ultimate triple revelation: the Word became flesh (appeared), he has made the Father known (exēgeomai, fully explained/revealed), the word of YHWH is now the Word who is YHWH. Samuel’s Shiloh revelations are the first sustained instance of the pattern that finds its permanent fulfillment in the incarnation.
